Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd put in about 21 hours a week, but it wasn't like a structured thing where I'd sit down at the same time every day. I'd mostly read through materials when I felt like it, maybe print stuff out or copy notes into a document. Some weeks I'd be really into it, other weeks I'd just skim through things. I never really had a system that worked consistently.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd go through phases where I'd study more regularly, but then something would come up or I'd just lose interest and my schedule would fall apart. Deadlines were the main thing that got me moving, to be honest. If there wasn't something due soon, I'd probably be doing other things instead of studying.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered there was something I needed to check, like if a deadline was coming up or if I got a notification. Sometimes I'd log in just to browse around and see what was new, even if I didn't really do much once I was there. It was kind of automatic at some point, like checking social media but less interesting.
Q4: Yeah, I guess logging in became somewhat routine after a while. I'd usually check it in the morning with my coffee, just scroll through to see if anything needed my attention. It wasn't really a deep engagement thing though, more like a habit I developed without thinking about it too much. The frequency was there but the depth wasn't always.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlight things, take notes in my own way. The structured text-based resources made more sense to my brain than videos or interactive stuff. I appreciated when things were clearly laid out in writing.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the video content, to be honest. I'd let them play sometimes but I wasn't really watching, just had them running in the background. Discussion forums felt like a chore too, so I only posted when I absolutely had to. I knew they were probably helpful but I just couldn't bring myself to care enough to engage with them properly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course or understanding something I was confused about. If it felt like busywork or something that wouldn't show up on an exam, I'd usually skip it. I needed to see the point of something before I'd invest real effort into it.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If something wasn't required, my brain just filed it under 'not important' even when I knew it might help me learn better. Required stuff at least had the pressure of consequences attached to it. Without that external push, I just couldn't motivate myself to do extra work.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download readings and work through them on my tablet without being connected. I'd also use external websites and YouTube videos to understand concepts better when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My notes app is full of stuff that the LMS would never know about.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd print out lecture notes and read them on the bus, or I'd review my handwritten notes before bed. Sometimes I'd discuss course concepts with friends over text or in person. All that learning happened completely off the radar.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a PDF or save a page once and then never open it on the LMS again. I preferred working with local files because I could annotate them my own way and didn't need internet. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have spent hours with that material offline afterward.
Q12: There were stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care about the course. Nothing specific was happening, I just felt disconnected from the whole thing. The moderate anxiety I deal with sometimes made it hard to even start tasks because I'd overthink everything. During those times, I'd log in but not really do anything meaningful.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. Even though my stress was generally low, I'd get mentally tired and just not want to deal with coursework. When I had other things going on in life, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. I had the resources and time, but sometimes the mental energy just wasn't there.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My eyes would move across the page but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to get through them without actually learning anything. The activity was there but the engagement wasn't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is probably why my completion rate was so low. Even when I knew something was important, I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then later never came. It wasn't that I couldn't do the work, I just kept putting it off until it was too late. That surprised me about myself, honestly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me actually submit work. I'd cram everything into the last possible moment, which meant my LMS activity would spike right before due dates. Without deadlines, I'd just drift along without producing anything concrete. They were stressful but necessary for me.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. I'd read it, feel whatever emotion it triggered, and then continue doing things the same way. Even positive feedback didn't motivate me to do more. It's like the connection between feedback and future action was broken in my brain somehow.
Q18: The course structure gave me a lot of freedom, which sounds good but was actually bad for someone like me. I needed more external pressure to stay on track. When things were spread out and self-paced, I'd just drift and not complete stuff. A tighter structure probably would have helped me actually finish assignments.
Q19: Having 18 online courses meant everything kind of blurred together. The course design was fine, but when you're juggling that many classes, nothing feels special or urgent. Things that made engagement easier were clear deadlines and straightforward instructions. Anything ambiguous or optional just got ignored.
Q20: That actually sounds like me. I'd log in frequently, browse around, watch some videos, read some stuff, but then never actually submit the assignments. I think it's because engaging with content feels low-stakes and easy, but submitting work feels like a commitment and opens you up to judgment. The activity is comfortable, the output is scary.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone's smart and studies efficiently offline, they might not need to spend much time on the LMS. They download what they need, learn it their own way, and show up for exams prepared. The LMS is just a delivery system at that point, not where the real learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're online and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do while walking around. They capture presence but not comprehension or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials, it's being mentally present and invested. For me, I was rarely truly engaged even when I was technically participating. There's a difference between going through the motions and actually being there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was actually surprised by how the semester went. I started with decent intentions but just drifted away from actually completing things. It also wouldn't show the CBT sessions I did or how I was trying to work on my patterns. My internal struggle between knowing what I should do and actually doing it is completely invisible in the data.
